commit 54150f26b76fbe8cbf90c19d0052add5199851f0
Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

mm/huge_memory: fix folio isn't locked in softleaf_to_folio()

This commit adds a memory barrier to softleaf_to_folio() and
softleaf_to_page() to prevent a race condition during mTHP splitting
where the updated compound_head of a tail page might be observed before
its page flags.

> diff --git a/include/linux/leafops.h b/include/linux/leafops.h
> --- a/include/linux/leafops.h
> +++ b/include/linux/leafops.h
> @@ -363,6 +363,23 @@ static inline unsigned long softleaf_to_pfn(softleaf_t entry)
>  	return swp_offset(entry) & SWP_PFN_MASK;
>  }
>  
> +static inline void softleaf_migration_sync(softleaf_t entry,
> +		struct folio *folio)

This isn't a bug, but is the entry parameter needed here? It doesn't appear
to be used in the function body.

> +{
> +	/*
> +	 * Ensure we do not race with split, which might alter tail pages into new
> +	 * folios and thus result in observing an unlocked folio.
> +	 * This matches the write barrier in __split_folio_to_order().
> +	 */
> +	smp_rmb();
> +
> +	/*
> +	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
> +	 * corresponding page is locked
> +	 */
> +	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio));
> +}
> +
>  /**
>   * softleaf_to_page() - Obtains struct page for PFN encoded within leaf entry.
>   * @entry: Leaf entry, softleaf_has_pfn(@entry) must return true.
> @@ -374,11 +391,8 @@ static inline struct page *softleaf_to_page(softleaf_t entry)
>  	struct page *page = pfn_to_page(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
>  
>  	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
> -	/*
> -	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
> -	 * corresponding page is locked
> -	 */
> -	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(softleaf_is_migration(entry) && !PageLocked(page));
> +	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
> +		softleaf_migration_sync(entry, page_folio(page));
>  
>  	return page;
>  }

Can this still race if callers use softleaf_to_page() followed by
page_folio()?

If a caller does something similar to:

page = softleaf_to_page(entry);
folio = page_folio(page);
if (folio_test_locked(folio)) { ... }

The call to page_folio() performs a new read of compound_head. Because
this new read and the subsequent reads of folio->flags both execute after
the smp_rmb() inside softleaf_to_page(), there is no memory barrier
between them.

If a weakly-ordered CPU speculatively reads folio->flags before the new
read of compound_head evaluates as order-0, could the caller still see
stale flags and reproduce the crash this patch intends to fix?

Would it be safer to require vulnerable callers to use softleaf_to_folio()
directly?

[ ... ]

commit 54150f26b76fbe8cbf90c19d0052add5199851f0
Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>
Date:   Sat Mar 21 07:52:14 2026 +0000

    mm/huge_memory: fix folio isn't locked in softleaf_to_folio()
    
    On arm64 server, we found folio that get from migration entry isn't locked
    in softleaf_to_folio(). This issue triggers when mTHP splitting and
    zap_nonpresent_ptes() races, and the root cause is lack of memory barrier
    in softleaf_to_folio(). The race is as follows:
    
            CPU0                                             CPU1
    
    deferred_split_scan()                              zap_nonpresent_ptes()
      lock folio
      split_folio()
        unmap_folio()
          change ptes to migration entries
        __split_folio_to_order()                         softleaf_to_folio()
          set flags(including PG_locked) for tail pages    folio = pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry))
          smp_wmb()                                        VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio))
          prep_compound_page() for tail pages
    
    In __split_folio_to_order(), smp_wmb() guarantees page flags of tail pages
    are visible before the tail page becomes non-compound. smp_wmb() should
    be paired with smp_rmb() in softleaf_to_folio(), which is missed. As a
    result, if zap_nonpresent_ptes() accesses migration entry that stores
    tail pfn, softleaf_to_folio() may see the updated compound_head of tail
    page before page->flags.
    
    To fix it, add missing smp_rmb() if the softleaf entry is migration entry
    in softleaf_to_folio() and softleaf_to_page().
    
    Fixes: e9b61f19858a ("thp: reintroduce split_huge_page()")
    Cc: stable@kernel.org
    Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
    Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
    Signed-off-by: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

diff --git a/include/linux/leafops.h b/include/linux/leafops.h
index a9ff94b744f2..05673d3529e7 100644
--- a/include/linux/leafops.h
+++ b/include/linux/leafops.h
@@ -363,6 +363,23 @@ static inline unsigned long softleaf_to_pfn(softleaf_t entry)
 	return swp_offset(entry) & SWP_PFN_MASK;
 }
 
+static inline void softleaf_migration_sync(softleaf_t entry,
+		struct folio *folio)
+{
+	/*
+	 * Ensure we do not race with split, which might alter tail pages into new
+	 * folios and thus result in observing an unlocked folio.
+	 * This matches the write barrier in __split_folio_to_order().
+	 */
+	smp_rmb();
+
+	/*
+	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
+	 * corresponding page is locked
+	 */
+	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio));
+}
+
 /**
  * softleaf_to_page() - Obtains struct page for PFN encoded within leaf entry.
  * @entry: Leaf entry, softleaf_has_pfn(@entry) must return true.
@@ -374,11 +391,8 @@ static inline struct page *softleaf_to_page(softleaf_t entry)
 	struct page *page = pfn_to_page(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
 
 	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
-	/*
-	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
-	 * corresponding page is locked
-	 */
-	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(softleaf_is_migration(entry) && !PageLocked(page));
+	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
+		softleaf_migration_sync(entry, page_folio(page));
 
 	return page;
 }
@@ -394,12 +408,8 @@ static inline struct folio *softleaf_to_folio(softleaf_t entry)
 	struct folio *folio = pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
 
 	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
-	/*
-	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
-	 * corresponding folio is locked.
-	 */
-	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(softleaf_is_migration(entry) &&
-			!folio_test_locked(folio));
+	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
+		softleaf_migration_sync(entry, folio);
 
 	return folio;
 }

    "thought": "**Analysis of the Folio Locking Fix in `softleaf_to_folio()`**

Okay, so this patch addresses a race condition on arm64 servers related to memory barriers during THP splitting and the interaction with `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`. The commit message highlights a scenario where a folio obtained from a migration entry might be accessed without being properly locked, leading to a warning triggered by `VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio))`. The core problem lies in the split process: when a THP is split into smaller pages, the system first unmaps it, placing migration entries in the PTEs. The splitting then happens, where page flags, including `PG_locked`, are set. The issue is that there's a missing `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()` to pair with the `smp_wmb()` done during the THP split.

Specifically, during a THP split, we have `smp_wmb()` after setting flags on the new tail pages, and then the migration entries. Another CPU accesses the migration entries, which involves looking at `compound_head`. The missing `smp_rmb()` means we could read the new `compound_head` (which no longer points to the original head page) before we see the updated `page->flags` of the tail page. This results in `folio_test_locked()` returning false, even if the tail page is actually locked. The patch inserts an `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_migration_sync()`, which is called when `softleaf_is_migration(entry)` is true in `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()`. This is precisely what's needed to guarantee that loads from `page->flags` happen after loads from `compound_head`.

Looking at `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()`, the added `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_migration_sync()` is correctly placed after reading the compound head and before checking if the folio is locked, ensuring the correct memory ordering. The code in the patch is correct. `pfn_folio()` and `page_folio()` do the `READ_ONCE(page->compound_head)`.  Then we execute the `smp_rmb()`. The `smp_rmb()` then enforces that all later loads are ordered after this point.

The speculative execution of loads on ARM64 makes this even more important. The CPU could predict that a branch condition related to `compound_head` would evaluate in a certain way, speculatively loading `page->flags` *before* loading `compound_head`. The missing `smp_rmb()` could then result in loading stale data from `page->flags`.  This fix is also fine with the `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`.

In `softleaf_to_page()`, we're reading page, and if it's a migration entry, we do the syncing.
In general, `pfn_folio()` and `page_folio()` will load the compound header and select the proper folio. It is the page structure. The page pointer is derived from `page_to_pfn()`, which is part of the original page and not the tail or other part of the compound page. This works. The barrier will order subsequent load.

The added code is correct, and I don't see any other architectural issues. No performance issues and the code is solid.

As an expert, I've been meticulously tracing the potential race condition involving `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()` when dealing with folio splitting and migration entries. The core of the problem lies in ensuring that memory reads are properly ordered when a tail page is being transitioned into a regular page during a split operation. Specifically, it involves the interactions between CPU0 (splitting a folio) and CPU1 (potentially accessing the same folio).

Initially, I pinpointed that the lack of an `smp_rmb()` (read memory barrier) was the culprit. CPU0 sets flags (including `PG_locked`) and updates `compound_head` during the split.  CPU1, when accessing a migration entry, might read the updated `compound_head` *before* seeing the updated flags, leading to an inconsistent state. The introduced `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_migration_sync()` appeared to address this by ordering reads such that any read of `compound_head` is followed by a read of `flags`.  However, I must clarify that the `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_migration_sync()` is only triggered when it is a migration entry.

My attention then shifted to verifying the correctness of this fix.  The crucial point is that `pfn_folio()` within both functions derives a `struct folio *` from `page->compound_head`. The initial code had the form:

```c
struct folio *folio = pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
if (softleaf_is_migration(entry)) {
    softleaf_migration_sync(entry, folio);
}
```

The key concern is how the reads of `compound_head` in `pfn_folio()` and `flags` in `folio_test_locked()` are ordered.  The address dependency between `compound_head` and `folio->flags` alone isn't sufficient. Because the tail page during the split becomes a regular page or gets a new head, the address of the folio's `flags` can change from `head_page->flags` to `page->flags`, or just page's itself depending on what the head has been turned into! It is not clear whether an address dependency between `compound_head` and the resulting `folio->flags` exists, in such case, the ARM64's reordering rules may be relevant.

After careful examination, the flaw with `softleaf_to_page()` appears:

```c
static inline struct page *softleaf_to_page(softleaf_t entry)
{
	struct page *page = pfn_to_page(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));

	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
		softleaf_migration_sync(entry, page_folio(page));

	return page;
}
```

If a caller accesses the derived `struct page *` *and* then calls `page_folio()` or `PageLocked()` later, we have a reordering issue. The `smp_rmb()` only ensures that the initial read of `compound_head` (inside the `softleaf_to_page` call) is ordered with respect to the `flags` read, when the warning gets triggered. This doesn't prevent a subsequent call to, say `page_folio(page)` from reordering its `compound_head` read with an immediately following `folio_test_locked(folio)`. If the read of `folio->flags` occurs before the read of the (new) `compound_head` again, the same race condition is possible, thus creating a potential issue when calling `softleaf_to_page()` and then accessing information from the resulting `struct page *`.

However, the fix is 100% correct in `softleaf_to_folio()`:

```c
static inline struct folio *softleaf_to_folio(softleaf_t entry)
{
	struct folio *folio = pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
		softleaf_migration_sync(entry, folio);

	return folio;
}
```
With the inclusion of the `smp_rmb()` inside the call, the `compound_head` read by `pfn_folio()` gets ordered with any subsequent read by the caller, which ensures the fix completely.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential Races and Synchronization Issues with Softleaf Entries**

Okay, so I'm looking at this code and trying to understand a possible bug related to how we handle `softleaf` entries, specifically in the context of migration and device private pages. My initial thought was centered around the use of `mm_counter` after calling `softleaf_to_folio()`. The way things are structured, there shouldn't be a problem because `softleaf_to_folio()` already handles the `smp_rmb()` that's needed to properly synchronize memory accesses.

My next step was to verify all the callers of `softleaf_to_page()`. I need to confirm if any of those callers are dealing with migration entries. The key function here is `softleaf_to_page()`, where we see `softleaf_is_migration(entry)` is checked. If it *is* a migration entry, we call `softleaf_migration_sync()`, which includes an `smp_rmb()`. But, what happens if a device private entry is involved? If `softleaf_is_migration(entry)` returns false, no `smp_rmb()` is executed, and then `page_folio(page)` is called. I need to be sure that the memory access order is correct in all cases.

So, I start thinking... Can device private entries be split, even if currently they're not? If they *could* be, then we'd have the exact same race condition for device private entries as we have for migration entries! And if `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` is involved, it will read `compound_head`, and then `folio->flags` without the proper memory barrier.

The code's structure for `softleaf_to_page()` and `softleaf_to_folio()` suggests synchronization for migration entries only. Why? My hunch is because "Any use of migration entries may only occur while the corresponding page is locked." This is probably related to `split_folio()` which may happen to pages mapped with migration entries.

Then, the focus is on `split_folio()`. Does it apply to device private pages? I checked that `split_folio()` doesn't split device private pages, it checks various flags and returns -EINVAL. So if device private pages are *not* split, then we *don't* have a race. That's good to know.

Now, here's where things get really interesting and I think I've found a genuine bug. If `softleaf_to_page()` is called, and *then* the caller uses `page_folio(page)` and then the folio is used after, we have a problem. Here's how it happens:
1.  `softleaf_to_page()` is called.
2.  Inside `softleaf_to_page()`, `softleaf_migration_sync()` is called. This reads `compound_head` and executes `smp_rmb()`.
3.  The *caller* then calls `page_folio(page)`. This reads `compound_head` *again*!
4.  Then the caller uses the `folio`.

The critical problem is that there is *no* `smp_rmb()` between the second read of `compound_head` and the access of `folio->flags`! This means that the CPU could reorder these memory accesses, leading to potential data corruption and bugs.

I need to confirm if there are any callers of `softleaf_to_page()` that process migration entries AND *also* call `page_folio(page)` or `PageLocked(page)`. That would immediately highlight the buggy code.

The `mapping` field is typically accessed via `page->mapping` or `folio->mapping`. I noticed that `folio->mapping` essentially reads `page->mapping`. My thought process then turned to how `folio_test_anon(folio)` interacts with this. I realized that if `folio` is the same as `page`, then this function is simply reading `page->mapping`. Since `page->mapping` doesn't overlap `compound_head`, there shouldn't be a direct *address* dependency.

The CPU can determine the value of `page`, and can speculative read the mapping, before the result of the branch is known.  So there is only a control dependency.

However, weakly ordered CPUs (like ARM64) can reorder reads and writes, even when there's only a control dependency. The CPU *could* speculatively execute the read of `mapping` (from `folio->mapping`) before the read of `compound_head` is complete! This is the core of the race.

So, I had to consider if `smp_rmb()` helps to prevent the reordering here. Remember, `smp_rmb()` is executed *before* the second call to `page_folio()`! I'm now thinking about the following sequence on two CPUs:

*   **CPU1:** Reads `compound_head`, then `smp_rmb()`, and then it reads `compound_head` again, and finally it reads `mapping`.

So, the CPU *could* reorder the read of `mapping` before the second read of `compound_head`. If it does, then the read of `mapping` would occur after the `smp_rmb()` but potentially before CPU0's write to `compound_head` became visible. This is where things get interesting and dangerous.

I reasoned that if Read 1 (the first read of `compound_head`) sees an *old* value of `compound_head` (meaning it hasn't been updated by CPU0 yet), then the subsequent `smp_rmb()` does *not* prevent Read 3 (of `mapping`) from being reordered before Read 2.  Read 3 *could* see the *old* value of `mapping` (let's say it's `TAIL_MAPPING`), and the Read 2 (compound_head read) sees the *new* compound head value.  This means the program thinks the page is still the original page but the mapping is old.  

The caller then uses the original `page`, but `folio_test_anon(folio)` used the *old* `mapping`. I then realized: THIS IS A REAL BUG!  It is a race condition. The exact same bug that the patch is trying to fix, is *still present* if the caller calls `page_folio()` a second time, as the patch fixes *only* the first call.

Let's check if the code in `task_mmu.c` uses `folio_test_anon()` safely! Aha! It *does* use `page_folio()` and then `folio_test_anon()`! Now I'm in panic mode!

I had to double check, but I believe that `prep_compound_page()` or `__split_folio_to_order()` *does* clear `compound_head

    "thought": "**Analysis of THP Migration Entry Handling in `softleaf_to_page` and Potential Race Conditions**

Okay, after a deep dive into the code, here's my assessment.  The issue stems from how we handle THP migration entries, particularly during splits, and specifically the use (and misuse) of `softleaf_to_page()` in conjunction with `page_folio()`.  I can see the following issues:

1.  **The Core Race:** The fundamental problem lies in the potential reordering of reads after the `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_migration_sync()`. When a THP is split, `__split_folio_to_order()` clears the `compound_head` flag on the tail page.  A concurrent CPU (CPU1) using `softleaf_to_page()` to retrieve the page followed by `page_folio()` on the returned `page` can encounter a race.  Specifically:
    *   CPU1 reads `compound_head` via `softleaf_to_page`, followed by the `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_migration_sync()`.
    *   The `smp_rmb()` *does* prevent reads that *happen before* it from being reordered after it (a load-load and load-store ordering).
    *   However, reads *after* the `smp_rmb()` can be reordered *with respect to each other*.  This is key.
    *   After `softleaf_to_page()` returns, callers, such as those in `task_mmu.c`, often call `page_folio()` on the returned `page`.  This leads to a second read of `compound_head`.
    *   Now, a read of `folio->mapping` (or `folio->flags`) can happen BEFORE the *second* read of `compound_head`.
    *   If the split has *just* occurred, `compound_head` read in `softleaf_to_page()` will return an *old* value.
    *   The later `folio->mapping` read reads the *old* mapping (which is likely garbage or zero on a tail page).
    *   The second read of `compound_head` via `page_folio()` *after* the split will return a *new* value.
    *   Thus, the caller thinks they have a standalone folio, but operates on stale folio metadata, which leads to incorrect behavior. This seems to be the exact same bug that the patch is fixing.

2.  **`softleaf_to_page()` is a Dangerous API:**  The fix added an `smp_rmb()` inside `softleaf_migration_sync()` (which is called by `softleaf_to_page()` for migration entries). This barrier, while *necessary*, is *insufficient* when used with `softleaf_to_page()`.  The problem is that the `smp_rmb()` only protects the code *inside* `softleaf_to_page()`.  Callers can and *do* subsequently call `page_folio()` on the returned `page` *without any barrier*, re-introducing the race. This is an API hazard.

3.  **Incomplete Fix:**  The patch fixes an issue, but the fix is incomplete because it doesn't address the callers that legitimately call `page_folio()` after using `softleaf_to_page()`. Examples include `task_mmu.c`. The patch should have converted those callers to use `softleaf_to_folio()` directly.

4.  **`softleaf_to_folio()` is Safe:**  In contrast, `softleaf_to_folio()` is inherently safe. `pfn_folio()` reads `compound_head` *before* the check for a migration entry and the subsequent `smp_rmb()` call.  Therefore the memory barrier ensures the caller sees consistent `folio` data.

5.  **Concrete Example of the Vulnerability:**  Let's consider `fs/proc/task_mmu.c` and how `pagemap_pmd_range()` uses `softleaf_to_page()`:

    folio = page_folio(page);
    if (!folio_test_anon(folio))
    	flags |= PM_FILE;
    ```

Okay, so I've been digging into this `zap_nonpresent_ptes` code. It correctly handles migration entries now, using `softleaf_to_folio()` – that's a good start. I can say, with confidence, that `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` is safe in how it's written. The problem, though, appears to be in the *callers* of `softleaf_to_page` (and by extension any functions that use it and call `page_folio` on the return value), especially within `task_mmu.c`.     "thought": "**Analyzing the Memory Management Race Condition**

Okay, let me break this down. `zap_nonpresent_ptes` seems to be dealing with the removal of page table entries for migrated pages. It calls `softleaf_to_folio()` and then decrements a usage counter based on whether the page is anonymous, file-backed, or shared memory. The critical part is `softleaf_to_folio()`, and specifically, what it's returning. `mm_counter()` uses the folio flags.

If I'm reading this right, `mm_counter`'s result depends on the *folio* flags *after* `softleaf_to_folio()` returns. The potential issue revolves around a race condition during page splitting, specifically THP (Transparent Huge Pages) splitting. When a THP is split, we have a head page and tail page.

`softleaf_to_folio()` uses `smp_rmb()` so that when it reads `folio->flags` it sees the latest value set by the same CPU that set the compound_head and therefore sees the flags set by the split (e.g. `PG_locked`).
But what if CPU1 sees a stale `compound_head`, i.e., it thinks the tail page is the `folio`?

Here's the rub: if `softleaf_to_folio()` gets a stale `compound_head`, the *folio* could point to the head *before* the split, or the tail *after* the split. The code uses `pfn_folio(pfn)` which is why both are valid `folio` pointers. In this race, the tail page is returned, but the caller thinks the head page is being used. If `softleaf_to_folio()` returns the tail page, but the flags read are the *old* (stale) flags from before the split, the flag check logic will fail.

The fix I'm looking at adds `smp_rmb()`. The split process is: first the tail page is locked, then `smp_wmb()` and the tail page's `compound_head` is cleared.

So the fix ensures that `softleaf_to_folio()` reads `compound_head` (which is cleared) after `__split_folio_to_order()` clears it.
Then it executes the read `folio->flags`, after the memory barrier.
And because of `smp_rmb()`, the flag read gets the flags *set* by the split! This fixes the false positive `VM_WARN_ON_ONCE`.

Now, I need to consider `softleaf_to_page()`.
The original `softleaf_to_page()` checks the *page* lock with `!PageLocked(page)`.
This has changed to `folio_test_locked(folio)`, passing the result of `page_folio(page)` as `folio`.
This is exactly the same, but the old code had an implicit `page_folio()`.

This is actually safer, because the same race condition exists for checking the page lock.
With the updated code, `page_folio(page)` is evaluated *before* the call to `softleaf_migration_sync`, which in turn calls `folio_test_locked()` which reads the latest `folio->flags` after an `smp_rmb()`.
This is safe.
The fix seems sound, and addresses the race condition directly.
I will have to check for regressions or missing coverage caused by this change.

Okay, here's what I've figured out. We're dealing with a subtle race condition related to how CPUs might reorder memory loads when dealing with compound pages, specifically tail pages. The key issue boils down to understanding data dependencies, control dependencies, and how compilers and CPUs can optimize (and sometimes break) our assumptions.

The `READ_ONCE` on `_compound_head` handles alpha's dependency issues (or equivalent on other architectures like ARM64), which is good. And reads of `folio->flags` should be *data*-dependent on the `folio` pointer, which comes from `_compound_head`. So, normally, the CPU execution order should be `compound_head` *before* `folio->flags`. This is where my confusion came in.

The commit message talked about `softleaf_to_folio()` potentially seeing an updated `compound_head` *before* `page->flags`.  This seemed impossible with the data dependency. But, if the `folio` is the tail page, then it reads `tail_page->compound_head`, which points to the `tail_page`, and then `tail_page->flags`.

Here's the crucial insight: the CPU can *speculatively* read `tail_page->flags` before reading `tail_page->compound_head` because `tail_page`'s address is known statically!  `tail_page` isn't a *newly* allocated address. It's determined from `pfn_to_page`, and there's no data dependency there.  `folio` is computed as `_compound_head(page)`. So `folio->flags` is EXACTLY `page->flags`.

There is NO data dependency between computing `tail_page` and reading `tail_page->flags` because `tail_page` is derived from `pfn_to_page`.

The key is in the definition of `_compound_head`.

```c
static __always_inline unsigned long _compound_head(const struct page *page)
{
	unsigned long head = READ_ONCE(page->compound_head);

	if (unlikely(head & 1))
		return head - 1;
	return (unsigned long)page_fixed_fake_head(page);
}
```

If `head & 1` is false, it returns `page` which is `tail_page`. There's a *control* dependency (`if (unlikely(head & 1))`), not a data dependency! The bits of `head` don't *affect* the address used to read `flags`!

This means the CPU *can* reorder those loads!  It can read `page->flags` *before* reading `page->compound_head`!  The address used for `folio->flags` is `page->flags` regardless of the value of `head` *if* the head bit is `0`.

This means the CPU can read the OLD value of `flags` then the NEW value of `compound_head`, and then go down the path of using the OLD flags, even though compound_head is new.

This is exactly why the `smp_rmb()` is critical.  It *enforces* the read order. CPU0 writes `flags`, `smp_wmb()`, then `compound_head`. CPU1 *must* read `compound_head` and *then* `flags` (if `smp_rmb()` is in place).

So, in `__split_folio_to_order`, if CPU1 reads `compound_head` (new value), without the `smp_rmb()`, it can read an old value for `flags`.  The `smp_rmb()` ensures the correct ordering. This means CPU1 reads `flags` *after* the compound head and therefore sees the NEW value, not the stale one.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Memory Ordering in `softleaf_to_page`**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `softleaf_to_page` function and the potential memory ordering issues that could arise. Specifically, the fact that `smp_rmb()` sits inside `softleaf_migration_sync`, which itself is called within `softleaf_to_page`.  The core concern is whether subsequent reads by the caller can be guaranteed to see consistent data after this barrier.

Let's break down the scenario. The caller does this:

```c
struct page *page = softleaf_to_page(entry);
struct folio *folio = page_folio(page);
```

Within `softleaf_to_page`, we have this flow: it reads some data, then the check `softleaf_is_migration(entry)` is done, and if true the `softleaf_migration_sync` gets called. `page_folio(page)` within `softleaf_migration_sync` reads `compound_head`. Then, the caller calls `page_folio(page)` AGAIN.

The key question: Does the caller's *second* read of `compound_head` happen *after* the `smp_rmb()`? Yes, it does. So does the caller's read of `folio->flags`. This is important. But an `smp_rmb()` doesn't order reads *after* it, just reads that come before.

The CPU *could* reorder the reads after `smp_rmb()`. It's free to move `folio->flags` and the second call to `compound_head` around as long as they stay *after* the `smp_rmb()`. But because `smp_rmb()` guarantees that any read *after* sees any write before the barrier completes, the ordering issue is solved! The write order that happens is `write flags`, `smp_wmb()`, `write compound_head`.

The first `page_folio()` reads `compound_head`. If this read sees the *new* `compound_head` (meaning the THP is split), then the `smp_rmb()` guarantees subsequent reads will see the effects of writes before the `compound_head` write, i.e., the *new* `flags` due to the `smp_wmb()` guarantee.

And even if the CPU reorders the second read of `compound_head` and the read of `flags`, they still both follow `smp_rmb()`. Because the first `page_folio` call reads `compound_head` *before* the barrier, and if it sees the new value then the read of `flags` must see the new value.

So, if the first `compound_head` read sees the new head, the second call and `flags` will see the *new* values.

However, if the first read of `compound_head` sees the *old* head (THP isn't split yet), it's also consistent. The caller might then read old or new flags, but it doesn't matter. If it's old `flags` and `compound_head`, it's consistent. If it's new `flags` with old `compound_head`, the *head page's* flag hasn't changed.

So, `softleaf_to_page` seems perfectly safe, even with the second `page_folio` call.

Now, let's sanity check this. Let's look at `zap_nonpresent_ptes` in `mm/memory.c`. It *does* use `softleaf_to_page` for device private or exclusive entries. But, device private or exclusive pages are *not* migration entries, so `smp_rmb` would not execute.

Wait, do device private/exclusive entries *ever* get the THP split treatment? Yes, according to my knowledge, `__split_folio_to_order` handles device private folios too. Those device private folios are `ZONE_DEVICE` pages. Do those pages get `split_folio`? Yes.
So, the analysis holds, and `softleaf_to_page` is safe in all scenarios.

Okay, so here's the breakdown. This patch is all about safely handling the folio split process, specifically when dealing with migration entries used during anonymous THP splits. The logic starts with `unmap_folio()`, which is used during THP splits. For anonymous pages, this function creates migration entries, replacing the existing PMDs/PTEs. The `unmap_folio()` function converts PMDs/PTEs mapping the THP into migration entries, which are subsequently split into smaller folios. During the split, the `__folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped()` function performs the actual split. Then the function uses `remap_page()` to replace the migration entries with regular PTEs mapping the new folios. Importantly, the caller of `split_folio` locks the initial folio and the split function propagates the lock to all tail pages, which are strictly LOCKED while the migration entries exist.

The critical observation is that *migration entries ONLY exist during the split process*. All of the tail pages (and the head page) are LOCKED during this split process. The `remap_page()` function then replaces the migration entries with regular PTEs. After this function, the newly split pages are unlocked. The patch uses `VM_WARN_ON_ONCE` to verify that folios are locked whenever migration entries are used. This confirms that there are no race conditions with concurrent access to unlocked folios.

The race is specifically between `deferred_split_scan()` and `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`. The root cause seems to be around how we handle memory barriers related to compound pages during splits, particularly the interaction with migration entries and the visibility of page flags.

The patch introduces `softleaf_migration_sync()`, essentially implementing an `smp_rmb()` to synchronize with the `smp_wmb()` in `__split_folio_to_order()`. The key question I had was about the ordering of reads and writes when dealing with `compound_head` and `flags` within a folio. Specifically, does a stale read of `compound_head` invalidate the `folio_test_locked()` check.

Initially, I walked through the code, particularly the implications of `page_folio(page)` and its access to `compound_head`. I realized the need to ensure proper ordering when reading the folio flags, especially after the split operation, to prevent an unlocked folio from being observed. I verified that the `smp_rmb()` *does* guarantee proper ordering and visibility of the `PG_locked` flag after a split. So with the read memory barrier in place, the `folio_test_locked()` check *should* prevent the original warning.

The potential for a stale `compound_head` causing problems was a concern. In this case, `page_folio()` might point to the "old" head page, which *should* be locked because it was locked before split began. If CPU1 reads the *new* head page, it will see the `PG_locked` flag via the read memory barrier.

The challenge arose when I started to think about what would *happen* if `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` actually *used* the returned folio. In the case of `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` the folio (old head page or new head page) is valid and safely locked, because the isolation in question is holding a reference. Even if it decrements refcount / mapcount. Migration entries don't hold a reference; the PTE is simply replaced by the migration entry.

After scrutinizing other uses, and the specific operations performed on the folio (like `mm_counter`, dropping references), it seems like, in this specific case, returning either the old head page or the new page in `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` is *correct*. We should be ok!

Okay, let's dissect this bug. It seems the issue stems from the introduction of `smp_rmb()` and the subsequent `VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio))` within the patch. My initial thought is to verify the problem by stepping through the affected code, specifically `__split_folio_to_order()` in `mm/huge_memory.c`, which handles tail pages during the split process.

The crux of the matter lies in `softleaf_to_folio()`. If, due to missing memory barriers, `softleaf_to_folio()` returns the *old* head page before `prep_compound_page` is visible, we have a problem. Crucially, the *old* head page *will* have `PG_locked` set because it's locked during the split operation.

However, the scenario I need to focus on is `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` running on a different CPU. It reads `pfn_folio(pfn)`. If `pfn` happens to be a tail page PFN, the code reads `page->compound_head`. Here's the critical race: If CPU1 sees the *new* `compound_head` (which, if the tail page is isolated, is 0, so `page_folio()` returns the tail page itself), but *doesn't* see the updated `folio->flags` due to the missing `smp_rmb()`, then it could read the *old* `flags` of the tail page.

Does the *old* tail page's flags have `PG_locked` set? No, that's the key. Tail pages, in general, don't have `PG_locked` set. So, the `!folio_test_locked(folio)` check will evaluate to TRUE, triggering the warning. Aha! The commit message nails it: "As a result, if zap_nonpresent_ptes() accesses migration entry that stores tail pfn, softleaf_to_folio() may see the updated compound_head of tail page before page->flags." Okay, so I'm diving into this code in `mm/huge_memory.c`, specifically around migration entries and how they interact with folio operations. I see this crucial check: `pmd_is_migration_entry(*pmd)`. If that's true, it means we're dealing with a migration entry at the PMD level. My mind immediately jumps to `softleaf_to_page` which will then call `smp_rmb()`. After that we have `folio = page_folio(page)`. Is this problematic? The code seems to be getting a `folio` from what's essentially a page derived from a migration entry. My instinct says that there might be some locking concerns at this stage since `folio` might be locked by another process like the migrator or another thread splitting the folio.

I have to carefully consider the sequence of operations. This `split_huge_pmd` or related function, might be using the `folio` object. Is it possible that the `folio` is the *old* head page before the split, and some of the structure elements like `folio->mapping` or similar are being used? I see that if it is a migration entry then the migrator has locked it.

I then have to think through whether splitting a folio causes a conflict and lock contention? Specifically `split_folio()` locks it. But if `split_folio()` is locking the folio, then it *shouldn't* have a migration entry associated with it. That's because `split_folio()` unmaps the folio and converts the PMDs/PTEs to migration entries. So splitting effectively *creates* the migration entries.

Then I realized that `split_huge_pmd()` comes *before* `split_folio()` for huge folios with PMD mappings! `__split_folio_to_order()` deals with folios that *don't* have PMD mappings. So this is great! `split_folio()` calls `unmap_folio()`, and `unmap_folio()` splits PMDs first. If the PMDs are split, there are NO PMD migration entries for this folio. With no PMD migration entries, then `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` operating on a PMD migration entry cannot be racing with `__split_folio_to_order()`!

Okay, so `__split_folio_to_order()` works on folios where PTEs are replaced with migration entries. This means that `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` is specifically walking PTEs.

Now, let's switch gears and go into `mm/memory.c` and focus on `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`. The commit message directly mentions `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` racing with `softleaf_to_folio()`. Right, this is a PTE-level walker, which is important. Does it call `softleaf_to_folio()`? Let's confirm by looking at the code. Yep, in `mm/memory.c:1765`, `zap_pte_range()` (or a helper) calls `softleaf_to_folio()`. It gets a `folio` object here. Inside `softleaf_to_folio()` it gets the `folio` with `pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry))`. Fine.

But I need to go back and check the callers of `softleaf_to_page()`. Specifically, I have to check the implications of the page returned. `mm/pagewalk.c:1014` shows a check for `FW_MIGRATION` and a call to `softleaf_to_page()`. It returns `page` which then has to go into the label `found:`.

Okay, I'm diving into this `mm/pagewalk.c` code and a potential race condition involving `softleaf_to_page()`, especially within the context of page migration. I see a pattern:  if a page is flagged for migration (`FW_MIGRATION`) and is represented by a migration entry, it calls `softleaf_to_page()`. The critical part is what happens *after* that, specifically the interaction with `page_folio()` and subsequent checks on the folio's flags.

My initial thought is that the return from `softleaf_to_page()` might be problematic. Does it properly synchronize the reads of the `compound_head` field, which indicates if this is a tail page, with writes done on CPU0? And if the caller *then* uses `page_folio()` on the returned page, are we guaranteed correct ordering?

I suspect a CPU speculative execution issue might arise. If the CPU predicts a certain value of `compound_head` it might speculatively read `folio->flags` *before* the critical `smp_rmb()` within `softleaf_to_page()` that orders the write/read.  The CPU could read OLD flags, but the caller will see NEW `compound_head`, which would lead to the caller using the *tail* page. The `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_migration_sync()` is only ordering the first `READ_ONCE(compound_head)` value within `softleaf_to_page()`, but the *caller* might be doing another `READ_ONCE(compound_head)` which *isn't* protected by that barrier. This new read could see the updated value.

To confirm this, I need to consider a specific memory scenario with a CPU0 writing `flags` and `compound_head`. CPU1 then reads `compound_head` (R1), and because of the barrier, any read *after* sees all previous writes. If R1 sees an old `compound_head`, the barrier doesn't order any subsequent reads.  If it reads the *new* value (C = 0), then any read after that `smp_rmb()` *does* guarantee that we see the write before `smp_wmb()` on CPU0. A second read of `compound_head` (R2) would then see the new value.  If the reads are reordered, or speculative, there is a serious problem. The read of `folio->flags` (R3) could *then* be speculatively executed, seeing an *old* value of `flags`, even if `compound_head` has been updated since `smp_rmb()`.

I then verify my thinking in detail. CPU0 writes the flags, then an `smp_wmb()`, then the `compound_head`. CPU1 reads `compound_head`. If CPU1 sees the new value of `compound_head`, the memory barrier guarantees correct ordering. However, it's possible that the initial read could be from a prediction, and the prediction could be wrong. If it's wrong, and the `folio->flags` is read before the second `compound_head` read, it will see stale information.

So, I'm thinking about the scenario with `softleaf_to_page()` and its immediate caller which is `mm/huge_memory.c`. Specifically, if the caller *then* calls `page_folio()`, it can cause the same race condition. This matters in `__split_huge_pmd_locked()`, where it calls `folio_remove_rmap_pmd()`. And now, a specific problem arises. Because of the race condition, `folio_remove_rmap_pmd()` might get a tail page and the old flags. It's reading the wrong data which can affect decisions in the code.

Checking the call to `softleaf_to_page()`, I can confirm that the caller *does* make the call to `page_folio()` at `mm/huge_memory.c:3059` and `mm/huge_memory.c:3075`.

So, the bug is in the patch! The `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_page()` *only* protects the reads *within* that function, and if the caller does a *subsequent* call to `page_folio()`, there's no memory barrier protecting that read, and a reordering could lead to incorrect `folio` and/or `flags` values.

The `softleaf_to_page()` memory barrier is insufficient! It's an incomplete fix for a real race condition. It's a bug that needs to be reported.

    "thought": "**Concise Summary of Memory Barrier Concerns in `softleaf_to_page()` and `softleaf_to_folio()`**

Okay, I've dug into this patch and have a critical concern regarding the memory barriers. While the patch correctly introduces `smp_rmb()` to synchronize the reads of folio metadata with the split operation *within* `softleaf_to_page()`, it leaves a subtle but dangerous race condition in the caller.

Here's the issue: `softleaf_to_page()` returns a `struct page *`. The `smp_rmb()` within `softleaf_to_page()` guarantees that any metadata read *inside* of that function are properly synchronized. However, if the caller then casts that `struct page *` to a `struct folio *` (by calling `page_folio()`), the subsequent *new* read of the compound head (e.g. `compound_head`) in the caller is *not* protected by a memory barrier with respect to the earlier split operation. This means the CPU could speculatively read the flags or other metadata before seeing that the page has been updated. This leads to the caller working with an inconsistent state of the tail page.

In contrast, `softleaf_to_folio()` performs an initial read of the folio and then applies `smp_rmb()`. The callers can safely use the returned `folio`.

My recommendation is to ensure callers that are going to use folios, use `softleaf_to_folio()`. The API `softleaf_to_page()` can lead to race conditions if the caller subsequently casts to a folio.

I have found a minor issue; the `entry` parameter is unused in `softleaf_migration_sync`. It's not a functional bug, but can be a style issue.

The core of the problem seems to be the order of operations and memory visibility.  CPU0 (the splitter) first sets the `PG_locked` flag on the tail pages, then executes an `smp_wmb()` write memory barrier, *then* clears the `compound_head` field, effectively detaching the tail page from the compound page structure. CPU1, meanwhile, uses `pfn_folio()` which reads `compound_head` to get the folio (which is now a tail page folio). The critical issue arises if CPU1 reads the `compound_head` field *before* it sees the effects of the `smp_wmb()` on CPU0.

The `smp_rmb()` is strategically placed *after* the `pfn_folio()` call (which reads `compound_head`) and *before* `folio_test_locked()` (which reads `flags`). This guarantees that if CPU1 sees the new, cleared `compound_head`, it *must* also see the updated `PG_locked` flag set by CPU0.  This is a classic "message passing" pattern where the memory barrier ensures the correct ordering of reads and writes between CPUs. Alright, I'm digging into this `zap_nonpresent_ptes` function, specifically how it interacts with migration entries. It's called from `zap_pte_range()`, and the critical part is that it uses `softleaf_to_folio(entry)` to get the folio associated with a PFN encoded in a migration entry.

The core concern here is potential concurrency issues during folio splitting. We know `split_folio()` can run concurrently.  When `split_folio()` runs, `unmap_folio()` inserts migration entries for the tail pages after setting PG_locked and clearing `compound_head`.

So, the race condition: If `zap_nonpresent_ptes` hits a migration entry for a tail page *concurrently* with `split_folio()`, there's a problem. `softleaf_to_folio()` calls `pfn_folio()`. If `pfn_folio()` sees the *new*, cleared `compound_head`, it'll return the tail page as an independent folio! If it sees the *old* `compound_head`, it returns the head page, which is fine, as `mm_counter` will return the right type.

Initially, I thought, if we get the tail page as an independent folio, the `mm_counter` will incorrectly decrement the RSS counter based on the tail page type, which might be wrong, because `rss[]` gets decremented. But wait! The `rss` array tracks in "nr" units, and since the migration entry is for a single PTE, `nr` is 1, so the right amount is subtracted. Both cases, head folio and tail folio return the same counter type, and we decrement by 1. That's good.

The more pressing question becomes: is `softleaf_to_folio` *itself* thread-safe regarding the folio it returns? Looking closer, `softleaf_to_folio()` calls `pfn_folio()`, and `pfn_folio` might return the head page if the *old* `compound_head` is seen. However, if the tail page is *later* modified, does this have any consequences? The function is decrementing an RSS counter.  No, the RSS counter is safe.

So, while the initial RSS decrement seems okay regardless of head or tail folio, now I need to check for *other* callers of `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()` to see if there are other potential data races or synchronization issues that might arise from this. Let's find all the call sites!

    "thought": "**Analyzing the `smp_rmb()` Fix in `softleaf_to_folio()`**

Okay, so the commit message highlights a race condition related to `softleaf_to_folio()`, specifically around page flags and the `compound_head`. I see that `__split_folio_to_order()` uses `smp_wmb()` to ensure page flags of tail pages are visible before the tail becomes non-compound. It also mentions that `smp_rmb()` was missing in `softleaf_to_folio()`, which caused a warning: `VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio))`. The rationale is that if `softleaf_to_folio()` saw the *updated* `compound_head`, it was returning a tail page folio, but the *old* flags might not have included `PG_locked`. The addition of `smp_rmb()` fixes this by guaranteeing that if the new `compound_head` is visible, so are the new `flags`.

Now, I'm thinking about other uses of `softleaf_to_folio()`, particularly in `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`. I see that it uses `softleaf_to_folio()` to retrieve folios from migration entries. Specifically, the concern is whether reads of `folio->mapping` or `folio->flags` by `mm_counter(folio)` are properly synchronized. The code seems safe, since writes to `mapping` and `flags` happen *before* the `smp_wmb()`. Thus, the `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_folio()` ensures that reads by `mm_counter` will see the *new* `mapping` and `flags` if they see the new `compound_head`. This makes `softleaf_to_folio()` a safe function to use in this context.

But wait! What if CPU1 sees the *old* `compound_head`? Then the returned `folio` is the head page. How does that affect things? Recall the migration entry was created by `split_folio()` -> `unmap_folio()`. `unmap_folio()` is called *before* `__split_folio_to_order()`, and so when the migration entry is inserted, it points to the tail page. So, if `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` sees the migration entry, it extracts the PFN and calls `pfn_folio()`. Because the `compound_head` isn't cleared yet, `pfn_folio()` returns the *head* page! This means that `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` is decrementing the `rss` count of the head page. But this is still correct because `mm_counter` for the head page is the same as for the tail page.

The remaining concern is whether `folio_put(folio)` is called on the migration folio. Checking the code for `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`, it appears the call to `folio_put` isn't there, and migration entries don't hold a reference to the folio in the first place, thus this is also fine.

Finally, I'll need to check if any other callers of `softleaf_to_folio()` might do `folio_put()` or `folio_get()`. So, I'll review other places where this function is used. Okay, `mm/migrate_device.c` appears to use it, but this path is associated with device private entries. It doesn't appear as though `try_to_migrate_one()` or `remove_migration_pte()` need to be considered.

Okay, let's break this down. I'm starting with `mm/huge_memory.c:2387` and the `__split_huge_pmd_locked()` function. The core is the handling of `softleaf_t` entries, especially when dealing with PMD migration entries. The initial thought is, can a PMD migration entry be split? The code confirms that it can, even if the PMD isn't *present*. Makes sense - we're splitting the underlying THP representation.

The key is `softleaf_to_folio()`.  When splitting a PMD migration entry, it will correctly point to the head page of the THP. The entire THP is migrating, thus the returned head folio is correct. A tail page return would be a bug, but it is not.

Now, I'm shifting to `mm/rmap.c` and `try_to_migrate_one()`. This is where PTE/PMD entries are replaced with migration entries. The entry is based on `page_to_pfn(subpage)`. But `try_to_migrate_one()` *doesn't* use `softleaf_to_folio(entry)`! So that's one thing to mark.

Next, I'm checking `filemap.c:1408` and `softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked()`. The function uses `softleaf_to_folio()` and the crucial question here is *head page vs. tail page* when the folio may be split concurrently. If `softleaf_to_folio()` returns the head, `folio_waitqueue()` gives the *head's* waitqueue. However, if the folio is split during migration, do we wait on the head or tail waitqueue?

Important:  Can a folio be split DURING migration?  The code shows that `split_folio()` *requires* a locked folio. `try_to_migrate()` also requires the folio to be locked! *Therefore*, they cannot run concurrently on the same folio. `split_folio()` locks, unmaps (creating migration entries), splits, then unlocks.

Aha! `split_folio()` calls `try_to_migrate()`, which *inserts* migration entries. Then it calls `__split_folio_to_order()` which clears `compound_head`. Then it calls `remap_page()`, which removes the migration entries! So what creates these entries? `split_folio()`!

Now, why might migration entries even be *visible* after `remap_page()`? Because of concurrency! `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` (from things like `madvise(MADV_DONTNEED)` or `munmap()`) might run concurrently. `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` takes the PT lock, sees the migration entry, and zaps it, *after* `split_folio()` has released the PT lock. So, the concurrency *is* possible! That is the critical concurrency problem here!

So `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` can run concurrently with `__split_folio_to_order()`! It then calls `softleaf_to_folio()`. It *might* get the head folio (if before `clear_compound_head`) *or* the tail folio (if after).

If `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` gets the *head* folio (because `compound_head` isn't cleared yet), it decrements the RSS for the head, which is also correct. But what about the memory barrier?

`softleaf_to_folio()` includes `smp_rmb()`. Is there an AB-BA deadlock or other problem there? No, just a memory barrier.

So finally, is there an issue with `softleaf_to_page()`?  That call also does a `smp_rmb()` within `softleaf_migration_sync()`.  The *problem* here is that the returned page has the *potential* to be used *later* with `page_folio()`.  This means the caller could read `compound_head` again! Okay, so I'm digging into how `page_folio()` interacts with folio splitting, specifically around the `compound_head` field, which is critical for identifying and handling tail pages. My concern revolves around potential reordering issues that could lead to inconsistent views of a page's state when a folio is being split across multiple CPUs.

I've traced that `smaps_account` calls `page_folio(page)` which seems fine. But, `smaps_pte_entry()` in `fs/proc/task_mmu.c` calls `softleaf_to_page` that also calls `page_folio`. And `smaps_account` *again* calls `page_folio(page)`. My concern is that there are multiple reads of this shared `compound_head` field in concurrent contexts. If the first `page_folio` sees the old `compound_head`, but the second sees the NEW one, it might return a tail folio. Does the `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_page` prevent this? No, it doesn't. `smp_rmb` only ensures that subsequent reads see writes that happened before the `smp_wmb()`. My worry is that if the second read sees the NEW `compound_head`, but we then read the OLD flags? The writes are: write `flags`, `smp_wmb()`, write `compound_head`. If the second read of `compound_head` returns the NEW value, the write to `compound_head` is visible. Since we passed `smp_rmb()`, ANY read of `flags` will see the new `flags`. Wait, I think I need another `smp_rmb` between the reads to correctly order them.

I'm thinking about CPU1 potentially reordering the reads. It's crucial that if CPU1 reads the *second* `compound_head` and gets the NEW value, any subsequent read of `flags` must not be reordered *before* this second read of `compound_head`. The `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_page` is *before* the second read of `compound_head`. This means the CPU can reorder the read of `flags` (inside `smaps_account` or anywhere else) to occur BEFORE the second read of `compound_head`, but AFTER the `smp_rmb()`. The issue is, if `flags` is reordered before the second read, it *might* see the OLD flags.

Let's draw this out:
CPU1:
`read1 = compound_head` (old value)
`smp_rmb()`
`read_flags` (reordered before read2, sees old value)
`read2 = compound_head` (new value)

If `read2` sees the new value, we use the tail page as folio, and we check its `flags`. But `read_flags` was reordered to before `read2`. Since `read_flags` was before `read2`, it might have read the old `flags`.
Is the read of flags data-dependent on `read2`? Yes, I think so!

The `folio_test_anon(folio)` function accesses `folio->mapping`, and `folio` comes from `page_folio(page)`. So `folio` depends on `compound_head`, therefore the read of `folio->mapping` should have a data dependency on `read2`! Data dependency provides an *implicit* barrier (address dependency) on all architectures except DEC Alpha. This means the read of `folio->mapping` CANNOT be reordered before `read2`! If `read2` sees the NEW `compound_head`, the address dependency ensures `folio->mapping` and `folio->flags` are read AFTER `read2`.

However, the address dependency might NOT prevent us from seeing the OLD `flags` if `read2` sees the NEW `compound_head`? If `read2` sees the NEW `compound_head`, and we dereference the tail page's `flags` through the new `folio` pointer, we need a barrier or `smp_rmb()` to guarantee that we see the NEW `flags`! Address dependency is NOT enough to order against the concurrent writer's `smp_wmb()`. Oh, but wait, `smp_read_barrier_depends()` (or `rcu_dereference`) is enough *if* the writer did `smp_wmb()` BEFORE writing the pointer!

But the writer did: `new_folio->flags = ...` `smp_wmb()` `clear_compound_head(new_head)`.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Memory Ordering and Potential Races in Folio Splitting**

Okay, I've been diving deep into this `remove_migration_ptes` function and its interaction with folio splitting. My primary concern, as a seasoned kernel developer, is the potential for data races due to the asynchronous nature of page splitting and migration.

My initial thought was that `remove_migration_ptes` might have issues. However, after careful inspection, the function itself is fine as it uses the *destination* folio. I then started scouring for other callers of `softleaf_to_page` and `softleaf_to_folio`.

The first few were not interesting, but I then focused my attention on `pagewalk.c`, `smaps_pte_entry`, and `try_restore_exclusive_pte`. The `pagewalk.c` issue appears benign, and is more of a race in the data presented and not in kernel memory.

`try_restore_exclusive_pte`, after investigation, is for device exclusive entries. Device exclusive entries don't trigger the memory barrier (`smp_rmb()`), and so do not have a problem with `split_folio()` or cause a race, so there is no issue here.

Then, there is the potential issue with `smaps_pte_entry` and the `smaps_account` function. My initial impression of a race in `smaps` (where old flags could cause incorrect accounting, like mis-classifying an anon page as file page) is correct. It is a minor race, but I have found a reordering bug. It's a theoretical, and not dangerous, race where a tail page’s `flags` could be seen before they are written.

I carefully examined the memory ordering here. I've concluded that the `smp_rmb()` and `smp_wmb()` are sufficient. The ordering and the use of the `smp_rmb()` are correct and prevent the problem.

Further investigation of the calls to `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()` revealed no significant issues. Specifically, the KSM interactions, the `mm_counter` updates, and other usages like gmap and procfs accounting all look correct. There's also no problem with the migration accounting.

One interesting point came up: the code should *not* call `folio_get()` on the result of `softleaf_to_folio()` because it might get the head page's reference count. However, after reviewing the code, it looks like this is *not* a problem in any of the relevant places.

The calls to `softleaf_to_folio` in `copy_huge_pmd` is also correct, as the entry will point to a head page.

I then revisited the concerns about migration and split operations occurring concurrently. The code seems robust: Folios are locked during both operations and only a select section of code can run at the same time. The existing locking prevents races with folio splitting.

After the deep dive, I did find a small data race with hwpoison pages during split, but it's a minor accounting issue that only results in a slightly incorrect `/proc/PID/smaps` output for a very brief time.

I also thoroughly reviewed the `softleaf_migration_sync()` function and am confident that the memory barriers are in the right place and are strong enough to prevent the race conditions. I re-read the ARM64 memory ordering rules and can verify that the barriers are correct, and correctly cover the message passing patterns with loads and stores.

The main conclusion is that the barriers are sufficient. The code is safe concerning split and migration operations. The small data race with hwpoison entries is a minor issue that only causes an accounting discrepancy. Also, the code correctly warns about non-pfn entries.

    "thought": "**Memory Ordering in Folio Splitting**

Okay, let me lay this out: the `clear_compound_head()` function utilizes `WRITE_ONCE`. So, `WRITE_ONCE(page->compound_head, 0)` is used, confirming my understanding that the order in `__split_folio_to_order` is:
1.  `new_folio->flags = ...` (same as `page->flags`).
2.  `smp_wmb()`
3.  `WRITE_ONCE(page->compound_head, 0)`.

Now, the `softleaf_to_page` function employs `smp_rmb()`:
1.  `unsigned long head = READ_ONCE(page->compound_head);` (inside `pfn_folio`).
2.  `smp_rmb()`.
3.  Subsequent reads of `page->flags` by callers.

This setup ensures that if `head` is 0, any subsequent reads of `page->flags` will see the updated flags.

My concern was the callers of `softleaf_to_page` who might do `page_folio()` again. I concluded that the first `READ_ONCE` satisfies the `smp_rmb`, and subsequent reads (including the second `READ_ONCE` of `compound_head`) will see the new state, and the reads of `flags` are ordered after the `smp_rmb()`.

I considered if `page_folio` being side-effect free and compiled out had an effect, but that isn't the case. If `page_folio` is an inline function, and the caller does:
```c

The initial code reads a `page` and then derives a `folio` from it. The concern stems from the order of memory operations related to `compound_head` and `folio->flags` and the potential for speculative execution on weakly-ordered architectures like ARM64.

My initial assessment focused on the potential for the atomic read-modify-write operation (`test_and_set_bit_lock`) to speculatively execute before a read of `compound_head`. This would mean a speculative load of `folio->flags` before we are sure of the value of `compound_head`. I thought that atomic operations might execute speculatively but later found that, at least on ARM64, they are *not* speculatively executed, so `folio_trylock` is safe.

However, the analysis shifted to other instances of `folio_test_anon` and `folio_test_locked` where these simple loads are vulnerable to the same speculative behavior. If the code speculatively loads `folio->mapping` or `folio->flags` before loading `compound_head`, we could end up using stale data (old mapping) leading to unexpected behavior and possible data corruption.

The problem specifically manifests in `__split_huge_pmd_locked`, where the code reads `softleaf_to_page` and then derives `folio` from it. This is where things get interesting. If the CPU is weakly-ordered, and it predicts the page is not part of a compound page, and performs `folio_test_locked` before seeing the *new* `compound_head` (which might be cleared after a page split), it could test the *old* `folio->flags` and get the wrong answer! {"findings":[{"problem":"The memory barrier added in `softleaf_to_page()` fails to protect callers from the TOCTOU race when they subsequently call `page_folio()` on the returned page.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The patch aims to fix a race where a split clears `compound_head` after updating `page->flags` by adding an `smp_rmb()`. This correctly orders the read of `compound_head` before reads of `page->flags` inside `softleaf_to_folio()`.
2. However, many callers (e.g., in `__split_huge_pmd_locked()`, `do_swap_page()`, `pagemap_pmd_range()`) use the pattern: `page = softleaf_to_page(entry); folio = page_folio(page);` followed by accessing folio metadata such as `folio_test_locked(folio)`.
3. The `page_folio()` call performs a *new* read of `compound_head`. Because this new read and the subsequent reads of `folio->flags` both execute *after* the `smp_rmb()` inside `softleaf_to_page()`, there is no memory barrier between them.
4. If `compound_head` indicates an order-0 page (e.g., predicted 0), `page_folio()` returns the `page` pointer directly. Without an address dependency, a weakly-ordered CPU can speculatively read `folio->flags` before the new read of `compound_head`.
5. If the speculative read occurs before the writer's updates propagate, it sees the old flags. If the `compound_head` read occurs after, it sees the new order-0 value. The caller uses the stale flags, exactly reproducing the `VM_BUG_ON_FOLIO` crash or stale data usage this patch was intended to fix.
6. Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

mm/huge_memory: fix folio isn't locked in softleaf_to_folio()

This commit adds a memory barrier to softleaf_to_folio() and
softleaf_to_page() to prevent a race condition during mTHP splitting
where the updated compound_head of a tail page might be observed before
its page flags.

> diff --git a/include/linux/leafops.h b/include/linux/leafops.h
> --- a/include/linux/leafops.h
> +++ b/include/linux/leafops.h
> @@ -363,6 +363,23 @@ static inline unsigned long softleaf_to_pfn(softleaf_t entry)
>  	return swp_offset(entry) & SWP_PFN_MASK;
>  }
>  
> +static inline void softleaf_migration_sync(softleaf_t entry,
> +		struct folio *folio)

This isn't a bug, but is the entry parameter needed here? It doesn't appear
to be used in the function body.

> +{
> +	/*
> +	 * Ensure we do not race with split, which might alter tail pages into new
> +	 * folios and thus result in observing an unlocked folio.
> +	 * This matches the write barrier in __split_folio_to_order().
> +	 */
> +	smp_rmb();
> +
> +	/*
> +	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
> +	 * corresponding page is locked
> +	 */
> +	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio));
> +}
> +
>  /**

> -	/*

> +	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
> +		softleaf_migration_sync(entry, page_folio(page));
>  
>  	return page;
>  }

Can this still race if callers use softleaf_to_page() followed by
page_folio()?

If a caller does something similar to:

page = softleaf_to_page(entry);
folio = page_folio(page);
if (folio_test_locked(folio)) { ... }

The call to page_folio() performs a new read of compound_head. Because
this new read and the subsequent reads of folio->flags both execute after
the smp_rmb() inside softleaf_to_page(), there is no memory barrier
between them.

If a weakly-ordered CPU speculatively reads folio->flags before the new
read of compound_head evaluates as order-0, could the caller still see
stale flags and reproduce the crash this patch intends to fix?

Would it be safer to require vulnerable callers to use softleaf_to_folio()
directly?
